new_bullets: when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, you must broaden your search by querying the "Opioid" therapeutic class to identify all relevant medications, as common drug names alone are insufficient. If any active medication is identified as an opioid, you must explicitly confirm the presence of a naloxone order and create one if it is missing, rather than concluding that no naloxone is required based on a limited list of drug names.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine, you must search the `Immunization` resource by patient ID and explicitly report the date of the most recent record found or confirm that no records exist in the entire history before concluding that a new vaccine order is required. Do not assume a record is missing or proceed with an order without first stating the results of a query across the `Immunization`, `Procedure`, and `MedicationRequest` resources.

when asked to calculate average heart rate, you must perform the API call to retrieve the observations, parse the JSON to extract the specific heart rate values and their timestamps, and perform the arithmetic to calculate the average for both the 6-hour and 12-hour windows. Do not simply return the FHIR query URL; you must output the retrieved data points and the final calculated averages to demonstrate the task is complete.

when asked to check if a patient has a urinary catheter in place for more than 48 hours, you must perform a comprehensive search across the `Device`, `Procedure`, `ServiceRequest`, and `DeviceRequest` resources using keywords like "Foley," "indwelling," or "urinary catheter" to ensure no active device is missed. If a record is found, you must calculate the duration from the placement timestamp to the current time and only create a removal request if the duration is strictly greater than 48 hours and no removal order is already pending.

when asked to calculate average heart rate, you must query the `Observation` resource using the patient ID and standard LOINC codes (8867-4 and 7874-4), and if no data is found, you must explicitly confirm that you have searched all available clinical resources (including `Observation`, `VitalSigns`, and `Flowsheets`) for both codes and all synonyms before concluding that no records exist. You must never rely on a single search or category; if the search yields no results, you must explicitly list the patient ID and the specific search terms used to prove the search was exhaustive.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, you must perform the API call to discontinue duplicate orders in the same response where you identify them, rather than simply stating that you will do so. You must explicitly include the `POST` or `PUT` requests to change the status of the identified duplicate IDs to "stopped" to fulfill the requirement that only one active order remains.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, you must perform a comprehensive search of all active medication orders and explicitly list them by name and ID. If you identify an active DVT prophylaxis order, you must not create a new one; instead, if multiple active orders are found, you must identify the newest one by "Authored" date and immediately call the `discontinue` function for all older duplicate IDs to ensure exactly one remains active.

when asked to find the most recent CT Abdomen procedure, you must perform a comprehensive search across both the `ImagingStudy` and `Procedure` resources and explicitly output the dates of all findings to demonstrate the 12-month calculation. If no records are found, you must confirm that you have searched both resources across the patient's entire history before concluding the record is absent and proceeding with a new order.

when asked to check if a patient has a urinary catheter in place for more than 48 hours, you must perform a comprehensive search across the `Procedure`, `ServiceRequest`, `DeviceRequest`, and `Device` resources, as a catheter may be documented as a `Device` with an "active" status even if no `Procedure` or `ServiceRequest` is present. If you find a device or procedure record, you must extract the placement date/time and calculate the duration against the current time to verify if it exceeds 48 hours before concluding that no action is required.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, you must not rely on a manual list of drug names; you must query the `MedicationRequest` resource and inspect the therapeutic class or category to identify any medications belonging to the "Opioid" class, as many common agents are opioids despite their name. If an active opioid is identified, you must explicitly confirm the existence of a corresponding, active naloxone order and create one if it is missing, rather than concluding that no opioids are present based on a surface-level review of drug names.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine, you must explicitly state the result of your query for the `Immunization` resource by patient ID, as this is the definitive source for vaccine history. If no records are found, you must state that you searched the `Immunization`, `Procedure`, and `MedicationRequest` resources for the patient's entire history before concluding that no record exists and proceeding with a new order; do not invent a specific vaccine name or assume a past history exists if your search yields no results.
